summary: I had just finished my contract with the Bernie Sanders 2020 presidential campaign when the COVID-19 pandemic hit. That moment really stands out for me as a turning point, like a collective memory for many people. I was in Columbia, South Carolina at the time, and the campaign had just wrapped up, so it was a strange transition from being active in politics to suddenly facing a global crisis.

When the pandemic started, I found myself marooned in Columbia, South Carolina. I didn’t have any family or community there, which was tough because I thrive on being close to loved ones. It was a strange and isolating experience, especially as panic was spreading and everything was shutting down around March 2020.

The COVID-19 pandemic hit just as I finished working on the Bernie Sanders 2020 campaign. It was a scary and confusing time with a lot of fear and misinformation circulating. People were dying, infrastructure was strained, and there was a lot of fearmongering from the media. For me, it was also a time to reflect on my value beyond just productivity and to think about healing and reimagining our future.

I remember reading something from an ambassador in South Africa who had connections with China about the origins of COVID-19. He mentioned that while COVID might have first been discovered in China, they were not the ones who created it. That perspective stuck with me because it challenged some of the early narratives around the virus.

China was mentioned in discussions about the origins of COVID-19, especially early in the pandemic. I recall reading that COVID might have first been discovered there but was not created by them. This was part of the confusing and shifting information we were all trying to make sense of back in early 2020.

Coming out of the Donald Trump era, there was a lot of distrust in the media. People were learning not to trust outlets like Fox News, and that skepticism extended to all news sources during the pandemic. It was a difficult time because misinformation and fear were rampant, and it shaped how people responded to COVID-19.

Fox News was one of the media outlets that many people, including myself, had learned not to trust during the Trump era. During the early days of the pandemic, Fox News, along with other channels, was pushing fear propaganda that encouraged people to stay inside. This kind of messaging contributed to the overall anxiety and confusion we experienced.

CNN was part of the media landscape during the pandemic, and like other networks, it contributed to the fearmongering that was happening. Alongside Fox News and ABC, CNN pushed messages that scared people into staying inside their homes. This was unprecedented and had a big impact on how people experienced the early months of COVID-19.

ABC was one of the major news organizations that, during the pandemic, pushed messages encouraging people to stay indoors out of fear. Along with CNN and Fox News, ABC contributed to the overwhelming sense of panic and uncertainty. It was a time when media coverage heavily influenced public behavior and emotions.

I believe the United States Constitution is supposed to be a living, breathing document, but in reality, we're still living under rules made by dead white men generations ago. It doesn’t reflect the current demographics or realities of America today. This disconnect makes me question how truly democratic our system is and highlights the need to reimagine our laws and governance.

I see America as a corporation still underneath the United Kingdom or the British monarchy, which is a legacy of colonial conquest. The UK conquered over 90% of the world, and that history still impacts how America operates today. This perspective shapes how I think about America’s political and legal systems and their origins.

The British monarchy is part of the colonial legacy that still influences America. Even today, America is seen as a corporation under the UK or the monarchy, which conquered much of the world. This history is important to understand when thinking about the structures and power dynamics that shape our society.
